ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Kakori Kebab; closest defensible match is lamb kebab (FOODON_03302051). Dum Pukht Kakori is a specific Awadhi minced lamb seekh kebab preparation.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (lamb meat, minced, grilled) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor. No specific Kakori Kebab entry exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Kakori Kebab or equivalent minced lamb kebab preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Dum Pukht Kakori Kebab is approximately 150 g, equivalent to about 3 kebab pieces, as commonly portioned in Indian restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Mutton Seekh Kebab / Minced Lamb Grilled; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy Lamb Ground Cooked (NDB 17290) for fatty acid and micronutrient detail
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved source contains an exact entry for Dum Pukht Kakori Kebab. Macronutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (mutton seekh kebab / minced lamb grilled) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for ground lamb, cooked (NDB 17290). The input record's per_100g values (energy 253 kcal, protein 18.7 g, fat 18.7 g, carbohydrate 3.3 g) are consistent with both sources and are retained with medium confidence. Micronutrient values (calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamin C) are cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy ground lamb cooked. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water are set to -1 where detailed fatty acid and mineral data are not available in ICMR-NIN for this specific preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; analogous estimate based on high-protein minced meat preparation with negligible carbohydrate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Dum Pukht Kakori Kebab. GI is estimated at approximately 30 (low) based on the analogous category of high-protein, low-carbohydrate minced meat preparations (carbohydrate 3.33 g/100g). The University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) confirm that high-protein, low-carbohydrate meat dishes have GI values in the 20–35 range. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (30 × 3.33 × 1.5) / 100 = 1.5 g, classified as very low GL. Estimation basis documented per Veritas policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Saturated fat at 10.0 g/100g exceeds the 5 g/100g threshold, contributing to arterial stiffness and elevated cardiovascular risk; sodium at 300 mg/100g is borderline elevated.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Dum Pukht Kakori Kebab to occasional small portions (≤75 g) and request preparation with minimal ghee, as the high saturated fat content (10.0 g/100g) is the primary clinical concern for arterial health, compounded by borderline sodium at 300 mg/100g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (3.33 g/100g) and high protein (18.67 g/100g) support glycaemic control, but high saturated fat (10.0 g/100g) may worsen insulin resistance with frequent consumption.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dum Pukht Kakori Kebab is acceptable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions (≤100 g) due to its very low carbohydrate content and high protein, which minimise postprandial glucose response; however, the high saturated fat from lamb, ghee, and cheese should be monitored to avoid worsening insulin resistance over time.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 10.0 g/100g and trans fat at 0.33 g/100g significantly elevate LDL cholesterol risk; cholesterol at 66.67 mg/100g adds further cardiovascular burden.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Dum Pukht Kakori Kebab only occasionally and in small portions (≤75 g), as the combination of high saturated fat (10.0 g/100g), trans fat (0.33 g/100g), and dietary cholesterol (66.67 mg/100g) from lamb, ghee, and cheese poses a clinically significant risk for LDL elevation and atherosclerotic progression.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Minced lamb is high in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies inflammatory pathways (TNF-alpha, IL-6); saturated fat at 10.0 g/100g exceeds the 5 g/100g RA threshold.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit Dum Pukht Kakori Kebab, as the high arachidonic acid content of lamb and saturated fat at 10.0 g/100g from lamb, ghee, and cheese directly amplify joint inflammation; substituting with chicken or fish-based kebabs is recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dum Pukht Kakori Kebab is a refined Awadhi Indian minced lamb kebab originating from Kakori, Uttar Pradesh, India, and widely served in Indian restaurants across Singapore. The dish is prepared by blending finely minced lamb with aromatic whole spices including clove, cinnamon, cardamom, and mace, along with ginger-garlic paste, green chilli, fresh coriander, and kewra water. Cheese (paneer or processed cheese) and ghee are incorporated into the mince to enrich the texture and flavour. The mixture is moulded onto skewers and slow-cooked using the dum pukht technique — a traditional sealed slow-cooking method — often finished in a tandoor (clay pot oven) or over charcoal. The result is an exceptionally tender, melt-in-the-mouth kebab with a smoky, spiced aroma and a rich, buttery flavour profile. Nutritionally, per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 253 kcal, 18.7 g protein, 18.7 g fat (of which 10.0 g is saturated fat), and only 3.3 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate food. The high saturated fat content from lamb, ghee, and cheese makes it a high-concern food for hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, the very low carbohydrate content is favourable for glycaemic control, but saturated fat intake should be monitored. The dish is halal-certified and gluten-free in its traditional preparation. It is typically served as a starter or main course in Indian fine-dining and casual restaurants in Singapore.

confidence_reason: No single approved source contains an exact entry for Dum Pukht Kakori Kebab. Macronutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN (mutton seekh kebab / minced lamb grilled) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy ground lamb cooked (NDB 17290). The input values are consistent with both sources and are retained. Micronutrient completeness is partial; monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several vitamins and minerals are set to -1 due to limited published data for this specific traditional preparation.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, full mineral panel, B-vitamin panel, and moisture data for Dum Pukht Kakori Kebab (minced lamb grilled with ghee and cheese) are not available in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as a composite dish entry as of 2024. 2. GI is an analogous estimate (GI ~30, low) based on high-protein, low-carbohydrate minced meat preparations per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008); no direct GI measurement for Kakori Kebab exists in approved sources. 3. Ontology codes are approximate matches; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 entry exists for Kakori Kebab. 4. The input record listed 'grabfood' as data_source with provenance 'ai_generated' and confidence 0.7; nutrient values have been cross-validated against ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy and are consistent. 5. Cheese inclusion (paneer or processed cheese) introduces a dairy allergen; the dish is not dairy-free. 6. The ethnic_tags in the input ('Curry', 'Biryani', 'Naan') are not applicable to Kakori Kebab and have been removed from the output classification.
